source.title: The Future of Writing in A World at War

## Dieses Dokument

- Titel: The Future of Writing in A World at War
- Signatur: SZ-AAP/W-AA63.1
- Datum: July 28 1940
- Sprache: Englisch
- Objekttyp: Zeitungsausschnitt
- Umfang: 1 Blatt
- Schreibinstrument: Violette

- Hand: Stefan Zweig

pages.text:
2

THE NEW YORK TIMES BOOK REVIEW, JULY 28, 1940.

THE FUTURE OF WRITING IN A WORLD AT WAR

Stefan Zweig Talks on the Plight of the European Artist
And the Probable Form of the Literature of the
Coming Years

(Photo by
Eric Schaal-Pix.)

Stefan Zweig.

By ROBERT VAN GELDER

"THE artist has been wounded," said Stefan Zweig,
"in his concentration."
He rapped his breast
with the knuckles of his left hand.
"How can the old themes hold our
attention now? A man and wo-
man, meet, they fall in love, they
have an affair—that was once a
story. Sometime again it will be
a story. But how can we lovingly
live in such a trifle now?

"The last months have been
fatal for the European literary
production. The basic law of all
creative work remains invariably
concentration and never has this
been so difficult for the artists in
Europe. How should complete
concentration be possible in midst
of a moral earthquake? Most of
the writers in Europe are doing
war work of one kind or another,
others had to flee from their coun-
try and live in exile, wandering
about, and even the happy few
who were able to continue work-
ing at their desks cannot escape
the turmoil of our time.

"Reclusion is no more possible
while our world stands in flames;
the 'Ivory Tower' of esthetics is
no more bombproof, as Irwin Ed-
man has said. From hour to hour
one waits for news, one cannot
avoid reading the papers, listen-
ing to the wireless, and at the
same time one is oppressed by the
worries about the fate of near
relatives and friends. Here flees
one without home in the occupied
area, others are interned and ask
for freedom, others wander about
begging from one consulate to
another to find a hospitable coun-
try which would accept them.
From all sides every one of us who
has found a haven is daily as-
sailed by letters and telegrams for
help and intervention; every one
of us lives more the lives of a
hundred others than his own."

He spoke of external hindrances
occasioned by blackouts, by lack
of freedom of movement, by in-
ability to obtain access to re-
search materials.

"For instance, I was just about
to lay the last hand to my favorite
book on which I had been work-
ing for twenty years, a large and
really the first comprehensive
biography of the great genius
Balzac. Reluctantly I had to
abandon this nearly finished vol-
ume because the library of Chan-
tilly which contains all of Bal-
zac's manuscripts had been closed
for the duration of the war and
brought away to an unknown and
inaccessible place; on the other
hand, I could not take with me the
hundreds and thousands of notes
because of the censorship. Just
as in my case, for thousands of
artists and scientists work of
many years has been stopped,
perhaps for a long time, by purely
technical difficulties.

"And the internal difficulty—
what means psychology, what ar-
tistic perfection at such an hour,
where for centuries the fate of
our real and spiritual world is at
stake? I myself had soon after
completing my last novel, 'Be-
ware of Pity,' prepared the sketch
for another novel. Then war
started and suddenly it seemed
frivolous to represent the private
fate of imaginary persons. I
had no more the courage to deal
with private psychological facts
and every 'story' appeared to me
today irrelevant in contrast to
history."

He said that most of the other
writers he knew had experienced
this same distraction in their own
work. Paul Valery, Roger Mar-
tin du Gard, Duhamel and Ro-
mains all had confessed to him
that they could no longer concen-
trate on their work. "I would be
suspicious against any European
author who would now be capable
to concentrate on his own, his pri-
vate work. What was allowed to
Archimedes, the mathematician,
to continue his experiments un-
disturbed by the siege of his town
seems to me quasi inhuman for
the poet, the artist, who does not
deal with abstractions but whose
mission it is to feel with the
greatest intensity the fate and
sufferings of his fellow-beings."

Yet out of this war will come
vast realms of experience in
which the artist may work, and
Mr. Zweig paced the floor ex-
citedly as he talked of this.

"On each ship, in each travel
bureau, in each consulate, one
may hear from quite unimportant,
anonymous people the stories of
adventures and pilgrimages which
are no less dangerous and thrill-
ing than those of Odysseus. If
any one would print, without alter-
ing a single word, the documents
of the refugees which are now
kept in the offices of charity
organizations, by the Society of
Friends, in the Home Office in
London, it would make a hundred
volumes of stories more thrilling
and improbable than those of
Jack London or Maupassant.

"Not even the first World War
drove so many lives to such crises
as this one year, never human ex-
istence has known such tensions
and apprehensions as today—too
much tension to be dissolved im-
mediately into artistic form. That
is why in my opinion the litera-
ture of the next years will be
more of a documentary character
than purely fictional and imagi-
native.

"We assist at the most decisive
battle for freedom that has ever
been fought, we will be witnesses
of one of the greatest social trans-
formations the world has ever
gone through, and we writers be-
fore all have the duty to give evi-
dence of what happened in our
time. If we reproduce faithfully
but our own life, our own expe-
riences—and I intend to do so in
an autobiography—we have per-
haps done more than by an in-
vented novel.

"No genius can nowadays in-
vent anything which surpasses
the dramatic events of the pres-
ent time, and also the best poet
has again to become student and
servant of the greatest master of
us all: of history."

Mr. Zweig says that the one
thing he can work on now is his
autobiography, which will carry
the title 'Three Lives.'

"My grandfather lived a life,
my father lived a life. I have
lived at least three. I have seen
two great wars, revolution, the
devaluation of money, exile, fam-
ine. The period of the French
Revolution and the Napoleonic
wars, the period of the Reforma-
tion—they were times not unlike
this. No other times can equal
the change we who are of middle
age now have seen."

He commented that he was
once "the most translated author
in the world."

"My books were published in
Italian, Japanese, in practically
all the countries on earth. They
had, how do you say, universal
coverage. When Hitler came in
my books were banned in Ger-
many, now they are banned in
Italy, perhaps next week in
France. There were Finnish
large editions and Polish—no
more. Every fortnight I lose a
country.

"Oh, that is not important. So
long as they can be published in
one language, that is enough. And
I believe that over here you will
resist the death of freedom for a
long time. It is inconceivable that
liberty could be destroyed here.
It will be regained in France; here
it will not be lost."

Mr. Zweig is here only on a
visitor's visa. He intended to
leave shortly for South America,
where he will lecture. Then he
will return to England. "I cannot
miss what is happening there."
He said that he is writing his
autobiography as he writes every-
thing else—"four times too long."
"I write the first time to please
myself. I put in everything that
I think of. I am a contented writ-
er who can write all day and be
happy. So the early drafts of my
books are very, very long.

"On the other hand, I am a
nervous reader. I become very
impatient when any author—in-
cluding myself—strays from his
point. So when I read what I have
written I cut it in great chunks.
I chop and chop until there is not
a spare word, a sentence that can
be done without."

COMMENTARY ON MATTHEW ARNOLD POEMS

THE POETRY OF MATTHEW
ARNOLD. A Commentary by
C. B. Tinker and H. F. Lowry.
347 pp. New York: The Ox-
ford University Press. $3.50.

THIS handbook, instead of be-
ing, as the title would im-
ply, a commentary on the
poetry of Matthew Arnold taken
as a whole, consists of commen-
taries on individual poems. Crit-
ical opinion is seldom, if ever,
expressed. But every poem in
the final collected edition of
Arnold's works, or nearly every
one, is touched upon at greater
or shorter length. C. B. Tinker
is Sterling Professor of English
at Yale and keeper of rare
books in the University Library;
H. F. Lowry is Professor of Eng-
lish at the College of Wooster.
Yale possesses that Arnold man-
uscript which, strictly speaking,
is no manuscript at all, but a
collection of notes left by the
poet, and it is this collection
which forms the basis for the
present collaboration. Professors
Tinker and Lowry originally de-
signed their handbook to come
out as a companion volume to a
new edition of Arnold's complete
poems, but as this may not come
off the press for another year or
more they have let this book is-
sue first, since it can be used with
any edition now available.

The Arnold manuscript, or
Yale Manuscript, as it is official-
ly known, is "a volume of some
seventy pages of notes of the
roughest and most casual sort,"
with words, the authors add,
often totally illegible. The work
is, however, of the greatest in-
terest to students of Matthew
Arnold in particular and, even if
in a less degree, to students of
poetry in general. By compar-
ing the first drafts of poems in
the MS. with the final printing
the growth of Arnold's poetic
genius can readily be seen, and
perhaps also why Arnold eventu-
ally turned from poetry to prose
as offering more scope and a
sounder medium for the outgiv-
ing of a mind so profoundly re-
flective as his own. Many are
likely to wish that Wordsworth,
his great poetic flight ended, had
come to a similar conclusion.

Matthew Arnold had a distinc-
tive flair for narrative poetry
but lacked entirely the wild ro-
manticism so often displayed by
Byron, also a master of the
genre. This handbook is nowhere
else more illuminating and in-
teresting than in the commen-
taries on the narrative pieces,
especially "Sohrab and Rustum"
and "Tristram and Iseult."

Arnold used lavishly a book of
travels in Persia by one Fraser
and published in London in 1826,
a source little studied by Arnold
commentators. The sources for
"Tristram" were, of course,
close at hand, and a wealth of
detail could be readily assembled.
The changes, therefore, will be
found to be mainly for the pur-
pose of heightening emotion and
increasing poignancy. As all
know, Arnold clung to the tradi-
tion of the love potion, leaving E.
A. Robinson to be the first to re-
alize that the ghosts, love potions
and on of earlier writers were
but devices to objectify subtle
psychological states.

This is a scholarly and valuable
piece of work.

PERCY HUTCHISON.

pages.text:
The New York Times
Book Review
JULY 28
1940
Section
6

ADVENTURE IN THE NEW WORLD
An Ingenious and Beguiling Account of Columbus's Third Voyage

TO THE INDIES. By C. S. Forester. 288
pp. Boston: Little, Brown & Co. $2.50.
By PETER MONRO JACK

THE story of Columbus's voyages has
been waiting, you might say, for
Mr. Forester's par-
ticular pen. The
great story has been
told many times in
poetry and prose,
seldom with this ex
knowledge of navigation,
a lively sense of
past, picturesque char-
terization, and an easy,
open style that automat-
ically turns the pages
for you.

You will not, at
moment, find a historic
event that so readably
and irresistibly dissolves
into imaginative fiction.
Much preliminary re-
search has certainly
gone into it, but the
story is fresh as a
daisy.

"To the Indies" is the
third voyage, in 1488-99,
of the Admiral Don
Christopher Columbus,
Regent of the Indies,
sailing on the Holy
Name, attended by two
caravels. It is the least
glorious of his voyages.
Setting out in his scar-
let velvet, which he
wears even under the
equinocial sun, with his
gold chain and jeweled sword and dag-
ger, his pages, steward and squire—his
appearance of dignity somewhat helped
by the stiffness of his rheumatism—he
returns to Spain in chains. Española
(Haiti), Cuba, the Chailan Islands have
already been discovered. The course in
this voyage is to the southwest, still
looking for the kingdom of Kublai Khan,
or the land of Ophir, or the Garden of
Eden.

Columbus's dreams are fantastic. He
sees the miraculous hand of God every-
where, even inventing miracles quite un-
consciously to delude himself. But he is-
the best navigator in the world, and he
brings the Holy Name to Trinidad, named
by him for the Holy Trinity.

An odd character is on the ship, apart
from the crew and the grandees of Spain.
He is Don Narciso Rich, fat and 40, a
plebeian lawyer who had risen to some
distinction. Ferdinand of Spain had de-
cided that some young lawyer should
accompany the expedition to watch over
the royal interests and send back a se-
cret report. No one was more surprised
than Rich himself when he found him-
self saying that there was no reason why
he should not go himself.

Like H. G. Wells's Mr. Polly, he is a
middle-aged man fearfully on the brink
of adventure, one of the great adventures
of all time—the further discovery of the
New World. But the astute and legal
mind of this plump landlubber outwits
and outlives most of the swashbuckling
grandees who swagger their courage
and coats of arms. Even the admiral
has to give way to the good luck and
good logic of this unexpected hero.

Narciso the lawyer and calculator of
facts had been attentive to the naviga-
tion of the ship. He had studied the
new astrolabe, the method of learning
the ship's speed, the manoeuvring of
the sails. The admiral, from past ex-
perience trust-
ing nobody, has
to admit that
Rich is at least
honest; and
when the ships
arrive at Trini-
dad the lawyer
is chosen as the
man to take it
over in the
name of Their
Highnesses of
Spain.

Blundering
and grotesque
compared with
the neat move-
ments of the
crew, Rich does
well enough—
enough to stir
his secret sense
of adventure.
When the ship
has navigated
what Columbus
has first called
the Serpent's
Mouth into the
Gulf of Paria
Rich asks per-
mission to go ashore with the long-
boat to visit the new land to the west.
His journey to what we now know
as the mainland of South America, and
which he at least knew to be the delta
of the Orinoco, is an exciting and ro-
mantic adventure, with a touch of
"Treasure Island" in it. It is Rich, of
all people, who had thought to bring
lines and hooks with him, and they are
able to have
fresh fish. It is
he who kills a
huge alligator,
mainly through
good luck. He
counsels friend-
liness and bar-
ter with the In-
dians he meets,
exchanging
worthless ob-
jects for yards
of pearls and
ounces of gold.
He cannot pre-
vent the raping
of the Indian
women by the
hidalgos, but he
is the only one
who has any
conscience about
the matter.

What Mr. For-
ester stresses
and makes most
dramatic by
contrast is the
touching inno-
cence of the In-
dians, smiling
and trustful to the last moment of
Spanish treachery. So much is Law-
yer Rich entranced with this new-world
life that it is with reluctance he
has to give up further exploration for
the necessary business
of joining the Holy
Name on its journey
north to Haiti to ship
homeward the gold that
the Indians had been
mining.

The admiral, Colum-
bus, had left his brother
Bartholomew in charge
of the island of Haiti
as Adelantado, and a
pretty mess of stupidity,
inefficiency, savagery
and mutiny his brother
had made of it. The
Indians, unaccustomed
to working regular hours
each day in the mines
for the Old World's ne-
cessity of gold in the
name of God, have re-
belled, and even as the
Holy Name arrives a
frightful auto-da-fé is
about to take place.

It is very cruel and
horrible, as the Span-
ish lords ride down
and slaughter the In-
dians as they would
do so many defenseless
animals, with no com-
punction and with a
great deal of gratifica-
tion. Again it is Don
Narciso who suggests our modern hu-
manitarianism. He manages to send his
report back to Spain, a clear and alarm-
ing record of the state of the West
Indies.

He does it in time. Before he sails
himself he is kidnapped by a group of
Spanish insurrectionists from the Holy
Name still bent on going farther west
for more treasure. They did not know
it, but they were anticipating the later
voyage of Cortes. Rich is being used
by them as navigator—a tribute to the
knowledge he had gained during the
voyage—and he steers the captured
caravel around the south coast of
Haiti north through the waters (the
Winward Passage) between Haiti and
Cuba.

A hurricane wrecks the ship, and Rich,
the plump middle-aged lawyer, is the
only survivor, living on roots and raw
land-crabs until he tricks an Indian
canoe to take him back to Haiti. There
he sees the admiral in chains, his fel-
low-voyager back to Spain. They sail
back together, the plump lawyer by
now lean and browned, with a new
confidence and courage in himself,
and an intuition of the New World to
come.

It is an admirable tale, realistic
and romantic by turns, adventurous
and ingenious, and, I should think,
the most beguiling book of the Sum-
mer.

C. S. Forester.
(Globe Press Photo Service.)

Identisch mit Seite 2, jedoch mit aufgelegter Farbskala/Grauskala für Archivierungszwecke. Die Skala verdeckt einen Teil des Textes.

confidence_notes: Der Text ist sehr gut lesbar, da es sich um einen gedruckten Zeitungsartikel handelt. Die Struktur ist klar. Die handschriftlichen Annotationen wurden in den Notizen erfasst.
